title: Operating Systems — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Comprehensive deep-dive into operating systems at Staff+ FAANG engineering level. Covers process/scheduler evolution, memory management, file systems, I/O architecture (io_uring, DPDK, XDP), concurrency (futex, RCU, lock-free), eBPF internals, virtualization (KVM, Firecracker, gVisor), kernel tracing, performance tuning, OS security, AI/ML workloads, CXL memory disaggregation, and cloud OS platforms — every topic a Staff+ infrastructure engineer must own.
details:
Staff+ FAANG Interview Strategy: Operating Systems questions at Staff+ level shift from textbook OS concepts to architectural decision-making under real-world constraints. You are expected to: (1) Trace the full path of a read() syscall from application through VFS, page cache, block layer, I/O scheduler, NVMe driver, and back — describing cache hierarchy, NUMA effects, and interrupt handling at each stage, (2) Compare kernel subsystems — when would you choose io_uring over epoll? EEVDF over CFS? cgroups v1 over v2? XDP over DPDK? Defend with numbers, (3) Design for operational excellence — how do you debug a mysterious latency spike? Tune the memory manager for a Redis workload? Harden a kernel against container escapes?, (4) Understand FAANG-scale economics — every kernel optimization that saves 1% CPU on 100K machines saves millions in datacenter costs, (5) Connect OS to application behavior — Staff+ engineers know that application performance problems are often OS configuration problems in disguise.

Company-specific emphasis: Google focuses on their custom kernel (google-gentoo), cgroup v2 orchestration, borg/k8s resource isolation, and their deep contributions to BPF, KVM, and Go's runtime. Meta emphasizes eBPF (Katran, LPM trie), kernel live patching (Kpatch), their OSS work on BPF verifier and io_uring, and large-scale kernel deployment (millions of servers). Amazon focuses on Nitro hypervisor (KVM-based), Bottlerocket OS, cgroup v2 and CPU management for Lambda/Fargate, and their deep kernel tuning for DynamoDB and S3. Netflix emphasizes kernel tracing (flame graphs originated at Netflix), io_uring for CDN serving, and NUMA-aware tuning for memcached. Apple focuses on Darwin/XNU internals, power management, and their custom silicon (M-series) which blends OS and hardware optimization.

Key principle: The best Staff+ answers combine deep kernel internals with production data. Know the code paths, but also know what breaks. "We saw that THP compaction was causing 50ms jitter in our latency-sensitive serving tier. We set madvise and used MADV_NOHUGEPAGE on the hot path — this removed the tail latency spike entirely."

sections.title: Process, Thread & Scheduler Evolution
sections.description:
PROCESS MODEL · SCHEDULING · THREADING

Staff+ expectation: Beyond describing fork/exec and pthreads, you must understand how process and thread models evolved from Unix V6 to modern Linux, how lightweight threads (goroutines, fibers) interact with kernel threads, and how FAANGs configure schedulers for mixed workloads (latency-sensitive + batch).

Process vs Thread — The Kernel Model: In Linux, processes and threads are both tasks represented by struct task_struct. The difference is sharing: threads share mm_struct (address space), files_struct (open files), and fs_struct (root/cwd). Fork creates a new task with a copied mm_struct (copy-on-write). clone() (used by pthreads) creates a new task with CLONE_VM | CLONE_FILES | CLONE_SIGHAND | CLONE_THREAD. Staff+ insight: The overhead of fork is not the syscall — it's the page table copy (if COW isn't triggered) and the TLB flush on context switch. At FAANG scale, fork is avoided for latency-critical servers except for immutable-architecture patterns (pre-fork workers like Apache, NGINX).

Linux Scheduler Evolution — O(1) → CFS → EEVDF:

SchedulerKernel VersionAlgorithmKey Limitation
O(1)2.6.0 (2003)Priority arrays (active/expired)Interactive heuristics were fragile; unfair to interactive tasks under load
CFS2.6.23 (2007)Red-black tree, vruntime fairnessWake-up preemption could starve batch; latency-sensitive tasks need more control
EEVDF6.6 (2023)Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline FirstStill new — production validation ongoing at FAANG

CFS Deep Dive: Completely Fair Scheduler maintains a red-black tree of tasks keyed by vruntime (virtual runtime — actual runtime normalized by task priority). The leftmost task in the tree has the smallest vruntime and is selected next. Nice values: Nice 0 gets a weight of 1024. Each nice level changes weight by ~10% (nice -20 = 88761, nice +19 = 15). The targeted latency (default 6ms) and minimum granularity (default 0.75ms) control scheduling frequency. CFS bandwidth control: cpu.cfs_quota_us / cpu.cfs_period_us in cgroup cpu controller — limits a group's CPU usage to quota µs per period µs. FAANG practice: Use cgroup cpu.weight for proportional sharing, not cpu.cfs_quota (which can cause throttling-induced latency spikes).

EEVDF — The Successor: EEVDF (merged in Linux 6.6) replaces CFS for better latency. Each task has a virtual deadline — the earliest time by which it should run to maintain fairness. The scheduler picks the task with the earliest eligible virtual deadline. Why EEVDF? CFS uses vruntime fairness but doesn't bound latency — a task can be arbitrarily delayed. EEVDF provides bounded tardiness: each task's maximum delay is a function of its weight and the total load. Staff+ note: EEVDF also supports server-based scheduling (SCHED_SERVER) which lets userspace define scheduling policies for groups — critical for mixed workloads where some containers need real-time-like latency.

Thread Models at FAANG Scale:

  • 1:1 threading (pthreads): Each user thread maps to a kernel task. Pros: Simple, kernel manages scheduling. Cons: Context switch overhead (~100ns-2µs), stack memory per thread (8MB by default, 2MB with ulimit). Amazon's DynamoDB uses 1:1 threads with carefully tuned thread pools.
  • M:N threading (goroutines, fibers): M user-space threads multiplexed onto N kernel threads. Pros: Lightweight (goroutine stack starts at 2KB, grows as needed), fast context switch (~10ns). Cons: Runtime scheduler complexity, syscall blocking can stall all M goroutines. Google's Go runtime uses M:N with a work-stealing scheduler — each P (processor) has a local run queue; idle Ps steal from others.
  • I/O threading: At FAANG scale, the pattern is asymmetric: one epoll/io_uring thread per NUMA node handles all I/O events, dispatching to worker threads (or goroutines) for CPU-bound processing. Meta's caching infrastructure uses this pattern — 1 I/O thread per socket, 64 worker threads.
sections.codeBlock:
# Process/thread limits at FAANG scale

# Increase max PID (default 32768, max 4194304)
kernel.pid_max = 4194304

# Increase max threads per process
# pthread_create default stack: 8MB (ulimit -s 8192)
# For 100K threads: 800GB virtual memory (not all resident)
kernel.threads-max = 500000

# Reduce timer slack for latency-sensitive processes
echo 0 > /proc/<pid>/timer_slack_ns

# SCHED_FIFO priority 99 for I/O threads
chrt -f -p 99 <pid>

# CFS bandwidth control (cgroup v2)
# Limit container to 4 CPUs of a 8-CPU socket
# /sys/fs/cgroup/<group>/cpu.max = "400000 100000"  # 40ms per 100ms

sections.title: Memory Management: Virtual Memory, Paging & NUMA
sections.description:
MEMORY MANAGEMENT · NUMA · PAGING

Staff+ expectation: Memory management is the most common source of mysterious production issues. You must understand the full life cycle of a page: allocation (buddy allocator → slab → page fault), mapping (page table → TLB → huge pages), reclaim (LRU → kswapd → OOM), and NUMA balancing. Every FAANG has war stories about memory misconfiguration causing p99 latency spikes.

Virtual Memory Architecture: Linux uses a 4-level page table (PGD → P4D → PUD → PMD → PTE) on x86-64, with 5-level (PGD → P4D → PUD → PMD → PTE) on newer hardware (Ice Lake+). Each level reduces the virtual address range by 9 bits (48-bit VA with 4-level, 57-bit with 5-level). Page sizes: 4KB (base), 2MB (huge page / PMD), 1GB (PUD-level huge page). On ARM64: 4KB/16KB/64KB base pages with 2MB/32MB/512MB huge pages. TLB coverage: A single 2MB page covers 512× more memory than 4KB pages with the same TLB entry count. At FAANG scale, Transparent Huge Pages (THP) are configured per-application — enabled for batch (Hadoop, Spark), disabled or madvise for latency-sensitive (Redis, Memcached).

The Page Allocator — Buddy System: The buddy allocator manages physical memory in powers of 2 (order-0 = 4KB, order-1 = 8KB, …, order-10 = 4MB). Fragmentation: Over time, order-0 allocations fragment memory, preventing order-9 (2MB) allocations needed for huge pages. Mitigations: (1) /proc/sys/vm/compact_memory — triggers memory compaction (moves pages to create contiguous blocks), (2) /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag — controls THP defragmentation (always, defer, madvise, never), (3) ZONE_MOVABLE — movable pages that can be migrated for compaction. Staff+ insight: THP defragmentation causes the most production issues. A THP allocation (2MB) triggers compaction of up to 512 base pages — this can stall the allocating thread for 10-100ms. Recommendation: Use madvise for latency-sensitive services and mark hot code paths with MADV_NOHUGEPAGE.

~100ns
Main memory access (local NUMA)
~200ns
Remote NUMA access (+20-40% penalty)
~7ns
L2 cache hit

NUMA Architecture: Modern servers have multiple memory controllers (one per CPU socket). Accessing memory on a remote socket is 20-40% slower. Linux NUMA balancing (enabled by default since 3.8) periodically unmaps pages and triggers page faults to detect remote accesses — if a page is frequently accessed from a remote node, it migrates it. Staff+ note: NUMA balancing adds ~1-3% overhead. For NUMA-aware applications, disable automatic balancing (numa_balancing=0) and use explicit mbind() or numactl. FAANG practice: Most FAANGs pin processes to NUMA nodes and allocate memory locally. Memcached at Meta uses libnuma with strict local allocation — each NUMA node runs its own instance.

OOM Handling — The Full Picture: When kswapd can't reclaim enough memory, the kernel invokes the OOM killer. It selects a process using oom_badness() which considers: rss + swap_usage + pte_mapped * 0.25 / total_memory * oom_score_adj. oom_score_adj ranges from -1000 (never kill) to +1000 (always kill). FAANG practice: Set oom_score_adj = -1000 for critical infrastructure (monitoring, SSH, container orchestration) and leave default (0) for application workloads. Use memory.high (cgroup v2) instead of memory.max to trigger reclaim before OOM — this avoids process kills entirely.
sections.codeBlock:
# Memory management tuning at FAANG scale

# THP configuration for latency-sensitive workloads
# Use madvise mode — THP only for explicitly-advised regions
echo madvise > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo madvise > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag

# Disable NUMA balancing (applications manage NUMA explicitly)
echo 0 > /proc/sys/kernel/numa_balancing

# Increase min_free_kbytes to reserve memory for atomic allocations
# Default formula: 4 * sqrt(total_memory_in_KB)
echo 67584 > /proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes  # ~256GB system

# Reduce swappiness — prefer page cache reclaim over swap
echo 10 > /proc/sys/vm/swappiness

# Set OOM score adjustment for critical services
echo -1000 > /proc/1/oom_score_adj  # init

# Pre-allocate huge pages for databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, MySQL)
echo 2048 > /proc/sys/vm/nr_hugepages  # 2GB of 2MB huge pages

sections.title: File Systems & Storage Stack
sections.description:
STORAGE · FILE SYSTEMS · VFS · BLOCK LAYER

Staff+ expectation: Trace a read() syscall through VFS → page cache → filesystem → block layer → NVMe driver. Understand the tradeoffs between ext4, XFS, Btrfs, and emerging filesystems. Design the storage layer for a database or object store — including journaling, checksumming, and direct I/O versus buffered I/O decisions.

VFS — The Virtual File System: Linux VFS provides a common interface for all filesystems. Key objects: struct inode (metadata — permissions, size, timestamps), struct dentry (directory entry — maps filename to inode, cached in dcache), struct file (open file — current position, flags), struct super_block (mounted filesystem). Dcache: The dentry cache is a radix-tree-based cache of resolved paths. Staff+ insight: Dcache is bounded by dentry-state in /proc/sys/fs/. Path resolution can dominate CPU usage under high file-open rates (e.g., a config server reading 100K files) — mitigate with path caching at the application level or using openat2() with RESOLVE_IN_ROOT.

Page Cache: The page cache caches file data in memory. Reads check page cache first (cache hit ~100ns, miss → disk I/O ~100µs-10ms). Writeback: Dirty pages are written back by pdflush (per-BDI threads) when: (1) dirty ratio exceeded (vm.dirty_ratio default 20% of memory), (2) dirty expiry (vm.dirty_expire_centisecs default 30 seconds), (3) sync/fsync called. Staff+ critical knowledge: A sudden burst of writes can trigger writeback throttling — dirty pages consume memory, block allocations, and cause latency spikes for reads. FAANG practice: Lower vm.dirty_ratio to 5% and vm.dirty_background_ratio to 2% for latency-sensitive services. Use O_DIRECT for databases that manage their own caching.

Journaling Filesystems — ext4 vs XFS vs Btrfs:

FilesystemJournal ModeMax File SizeFAANG Use Case
ext4Ordered (metadata only)16TB (4KB blocks)Boot partition, containers (default for Ubuntu/Debian)
XFSMetadata (fast recovery)8EB (8KB+ blocks)Large-scale storage (Netflix, Meta — Object storage, Cassandra)
BtrfsCopy-on-write16EBSnapshots, subvolumes (Docker overlay2 with btrfs)

io_uring — The Modern I/O Path: Traditional read()/write() syscalls have overhead: (1) syscall entry/exit (~100ns each), (2) buffer copy, (3) context switching for blocking I/O. io_uring (Linux 5.1+) uses a shared ring buffer between kernel and userspace — submission queue (SQ) and completion queue (CQ). Key features: (1) Polled I/O — kernel polls NVMe completion instead of waiting for IRQ, reducing latency to ~2-5µs (vs ~10-20µs with IRQ), (2) Fixed buffers — pre-register buffers to avoid page pinning per I/O, (3) Linked operations — chain ops like read→write without userspace return, (4) Zero-copy network — send data directly from file cache to socket. FAANG adoption: Meta uses io_uring for their CDN serving layer (10µs median read latency), Cloudflare for their KV store, and database engines (RocksDB, Scylla) increasingly adopt io_uring.
sections.codeBlock:
# Storage I/O tuning at FAANG scale

# Reduce dirty page ratios for latency-sensitive workloads
vm.dirty_ratio = 5
vm.dirty_background_ratio = 2

# Increase dirty expiry to reduce writeback bursts
vm.dirty_expire_centisecs = 3000  # 30s (default)

# I/O scheduler — use none (NVMe) or mq-deadline (SATA SSD)
# NVMe drives are fast enough that scheduling adds overhead
echo none > /sys/block/nvme0n1/queue/scheduler

# Increase read-ahead for sequential workloads
echo 4096 > /sys/block/nvme0n1/queue/read_ahead_kb  # 4MB

# Disable access time updates (reduces metadata writes)
# Mount with noatime,nodiratime

# Enable polling for io_uring (high-CPU, low-latency tradeoff)
# Set IORING_SETUP_IOPOLL flag when creating io_uring

sections.title: Scheduling: CFS → EEVDF & Real-Time Considerations
sections.description:
SCHEDULING · LATENCY · REAL-TIME

Staff+ expectation: Beyond knowing CFS and EEVDF, you must design the scheduling strategy for a mixed-criticality system — one host running latency-sensitive serving (memcached), batch analytics (Spark), and background tasks (log rotation). This is the daily reality at FAANG.

CFS Bandwidth Control — The Deep Mechanics: cgroup cpu controller has two parameters: cpu.weight (proportional share, range 1-10000) and cpu.max (hard limit, format $quota $period). How CFS enforces fairness: Each cgroup has a cfs_rq (CFS runqueue) with a runtime account. When a group exceeds its quota in a period, its tasks are throttled (dequeued from the runqueue and placed on a throttled list). Throttling is atomic — all tasks in the group stop for the remainder of the period. Staff+ insight: This causes the thundering herd problem: when the next period starts, all throttled tasks become runnable simultaneously, causing a CPU burst. Mitigation: Use cpu.weight instead of cpu.max for latency-sensitive workloads. Reserve cpu.max only for noisy-neighbor containment.

SCHED_DEADLINE — The Real-Time Scheduling Class: Linux has four scheduling classes: SCHED_FIFO (highest, fixed priority 1-99), SCHED_RR (round-robin within priority), SCHED_DEADLINE (EDF — Earliest Deadline First), SCHED_NORMAL/CFS-EEVDF (default fair scheduling). SCHED_DEADLINE (added in 3.14) lets a task specify: runtime (maximum CPU time per period), deadline (relative deadline), period (activation period). The kernel guarantees that the task will receive runtime deadline before its period. FAANG use case: Audio/video processing pipelines, control loops for SDN, and real-time data processing. Warning: Misconfigured SCHED_DEADLINE can monopolize CPUs — the kernel enforces admission control to prevent SCHED_DEADLINE tasks from exceeding ~95% of CPU.

Mixed-Criticality Scheduling at FAANG:

latency_sensitive
Google cgroup — SCHED_IDLE excluded, weighted 100
best_effort
Google cgroup — weighted 50, can be preempted
batch
SCHED_IDLE (weight ~3), only runs when idle

Google's cgroup tiering (borg/gke): Google classifies workloads into latency_sensitive, best_effort, and batch. Each has a different cgroup cpu.weight, and latency_sensitive tasks are pinned to dedicated cores where possible. Batch tasks run on hyperthreads or leftover cores and use SCHED_IDLE so they never preempt other classes. Meta's approach: Similar tiering but uses their own OSS cgroup v2 cpu.weight tuning — latency-sensitive workloads get weight 100, normal 50, batch 1. They also use SCHED_AUTOGROUP to group related tasks (by session or process group).
sections.codeBlock:
# Mixed-criticality scheduler tuning

# Cgroup v2 CPU configuration for latency-sensitive workload
# /sys/fs/cgroup/<group>/
echo 100 > cpu.weight  # Latency-sensitive: high weight

# Cgroup v2 CPU configuration for batch workload
echo 1 > cpu.weight    # Batch: minimum weight
# Mark as SCHED_IDLE via cgroup
# The SCHED_IDLE class runs at weight ~3

# Pin latency-sensitive task to dedicated cores
# Using cpuset cgroup
# /sys/fs/cgroup/<group>/cpuset.cpus = "0-3"

# Reserve cores for real-time via kernel boot parameter
# isolcpus=4-7 nohz_full=4-7 rcu_nocbs=4-7

sections.title: I/O Architecture: Interrupts → io_uring → DPDK → XDP
sections.description:
I/O · INTERRUPTS · POLLING · DPDK · XDP

Staff+ expectation: The I/O path is where OS theory meets hardware reality. You must understand the full evolution from interrupt-driven I/O to polling to kernel-bypass to in-kernel processing (XDP/eBPF). You should be able to recommend an I/O strategy based on workload latency, throughput, and CPU cost requirements.

Interrupt Handling — The Bottleneck: Traditional NIC I/O: packet arrives → DMA to ring buffer → NIC raises IRQ → kernel handler → softirq (NET_RX_SOFTIRQ) → network stack → socket buffer → userspace read. Problems at scale: (1) IRQ affinity: All interrupts landing on one CPU cause that CPU to be saturated. Solution: irqbalance and explicit /proc/irq/*/smp_affinity. (2) Interrupt storms: High packet rates (10M+ pps) generate so many interrupts that the CPU spends all time handling them. Solution: NAPI (New API) — switch to polling mode when interrupt rate exceeds threshold (net.core.netdev_budget, default 300 packets per softirq). NAPI polls the NIC in a loop, batching packets, then re-enables interrupts when the ring is drained.

io_uring — The Kernel Accelerated I/O Engine: io_uring replaces the traditional syscall-based I/O model with a shared ring buffer. Submission path: Userspace writes an I/O request (READ/WRITE/SEND/RECV/ACCEPT/OPENAT/STATX) to the submission queue (SQ) and rings the doorbell (stores SQ tail). Kernel picks up requests, processes them, writes completions to the completion queue (CQ), and advances CQ tail. Userspace reads completions without a syscall (just memory ordering). Key performance numbers: Traditional read syscall: ~200-400ns (just the syscall overhead). io_uring with SQPOLL (kernel polling thread): ~50-100ns per I/O submission. With IORING_SETUP_IOPOLL (NVMe polling): latency drops from ~10µs (IRQ-driven) to ~2-3µs (polling). Staff+ insight: io_uring's SQPOLL thread eliminates the syscall entirely for submission and completion — a dedicated kernel thread polls the SQ and posts completions without any context switch. This is transformative for high-throughput storage services.

DPDK — Kernel Bypass for Extreme Throughput: Data Plane Development Kit (DPDK) bypasses the kernel networking stack entirely: (1) Userspace driver polls NIC directly (PMD — Poll Mode Driver), (2) Huge pages for DMA buffers (eliminates TLB misses), (3) CPU affinity — dedicated cores for polling, (4) Lock-free ring buffers between application threads. Performance: DPDK achieves 10-80 Mpps (million packets per second) on a single core (vs ~1-2 Mpps with kernel stack). Trade-offs: (1) Application owns the NIC — no kernel network stack (no TCP offload, no routing, no netfilter), (2) Dedicated CPU cores (1-2 cores just for polling), (3) Complex integration — must implement or embed a TCP stack (mTCP, F-Stack, TLDK). FAANG use case: Meta uses DPDK for their load balancers (Katran uses XDP now, but earlier versions used DPDK). Cloud providers use DPDK for virtual switches (OVS-DPDK).

XDP — eXtreme Data Path: XDP (merged in Linux 4.8) attaches an eBPF program at the NIC driver level, before the kernel allocates an SKB (socket buffer). Actions: XDP_DROP (drop packet — fastest firewall), XDP_PASS (pass to kernel stack), XDP_TX (transmit back out same NIC), XDP_REDIRECT (redirect to another NIC or AF_XDP socket). Performance: XDP processes packets at 10-25 Mpps per core — not as fast as DPDK but runs within the kernel, so the kernel network stack is available for passed packets. FAANG use case: Meta's Katran (L4 load balancer) uses XDP to forward 20M+ pps per server. Cloudflare uses XDP for DDoS mitigation (drops attack traffic before stack processing).
sections.codeBlock:
# I/O tuning at FAANG scale

# NAPI tuning — increase budget for high packet rates
net.core.netdev_budget = 600    # packets per softirq (default 300)
net.core.netdev_budget_usecs = 2000  # time budget in µs

# RPS (Receive Packet Steering) — spread RX processing across CPUs
# For each RX queue, echo CPU mask to /sys/class/net/eth0/queues/rx-*/rps_cpus

# RFS (Receive Flow Steering) — direct flows to application CPU
echo 32768 > /proc/sys/net/core/rps_sock_flow_entries

# XDP program load (example: drop all traffic on interface)
ip link set dev eth0 xdp obj drop.o sec .text

# io_uring SQPOLL — kernel polls submission queue
echo 1 > /sys/kernel/io_uring/sq_thread_enabled

sections.title: Concurrency & Synchronization: futex, RCU & Lock-Free Data Structures
sections.description:
CONCURRENCY · SYNCHRONIZATION · LOCK-FREE

Staff+ expectation: You must understand the full cost hierarchy of synchronization: atomic ops (~5ns) → spinlock (10-20ns under contention) → mutex with futex (100ns-5µs) → kernel-assisted (10-100µs). You should know when RCU is the right answer (read-mostly, large structures) and when lock-free techniques (seqlock, hazard pointers, EBR) are necessary.

futex — Fast Userspace Mutex: Futex (Fast Userspace Mutex) is the building block for most userspace synchronization (pthread_mutex, pthread_cond, semaphores). How it works: (1) Userspace atomically decrements the futex word. (2) If uncontended (word was 1 → 0), the lock is acquired — no syscall. (3) If contended (word was 0 → -1), the thread calls futex(FUTEX_WAIT) syscall to block. (4) Unlock: atomically increment futex word. If waiters exist (word was 0 → 1), call futex(FUTEX_WAKE). Staff+ insight: The uncontended fast path is ~25ns. The contended path adds ~2-5µs (syscall + scheduler). Priority inheritance: FUTEX_LOCK_PI implements priority inheritance — a lower-priority task holding a lock temporarily inherits the priority of a higher-priority task waiting for it, preventing priority inversion. FAANG use case: Real-time audio processing pipelines use PI futexes to ensure bounded lock-wait times.

RCU — Read-Copy-Update: RCU allows readers to access a data structure without any locks, while writers make a copy, modify it, and publish the new version. Key insight: Readers can proceed concurrently with writers — they may see the old or new version, but never an inconsistent state. Graces period: After a writer publishes a new version, it must wait for a grace period — all pre-existing readers must have completed. In Linux, the grace period is detected by quiescent states (context switch, userspace return, idle). RCU primitives: rcu_read_lock()/rcu_read_unlock() — these are almost free (just memory barriers on some architectures). synchronize_rcu() — blocks until grace period completes (can be 1-10ms). call_rcu() — callback when grace period completes (non-blocking). FAANG use case: Linux kernel networking (routing tables, netfilter rules), the VFS dcache, and several BPF maps use RCU. Meta's BPF-based load balancers use RCU for configuration updates.

Lock-Free Data Structures:

TechniqueMemory ReclamationOverheadUse Case
SeqlockSequence counterLow (~5ns read, ~20ns write)Time-of-day, stats counters; writer must be fast
Hazard PointersPer-thread retired listMedium (scan on reclaim)Lock-free hash tables, FIFO queues
EBR (Epoch-Based Reclaim)Global epoch counterLow (~memory fence per op)RCU-like, used in concurrent data structure libs
sections.codeBlock:
# Synchronization diagnostics

# Show futex contention (context switches due to mutex waits)
perf stat -e 'syscalls:sys_enter_futex' -p <pid>

# Show RCU stall warnings (kernel config)
# CONFIG_RCU_CPU_STALL_TIMEOUT=21  # warn after 21 seconds

# Lockdep — kernel lock dependency validator
# CONFIG_PROVE_LOCKING=y  # enable lock ordering validation
# CONFIG_DEBUG_ATOMIC_SLEEP=y  # detect sleep in atomic context

# User-space lock profiling (Google's gperftools)
# LD_PRELOAD=libtcmalloc_and_profiler.so
# CPUPROFILE=profile.prof ./binary

sections.title: eBPF: Verifier, CO-RE, Program Types & FAANG Deployments
sections.description:
eBPF · KERNEL PROGRAMMING · OBSERVABILITY

Staff+ expectation: eBPF is the most important kernel technology for infrastructure engineering. You must understand the full lifecycle — from writing BPF C, through the verifier, JIT compilation, map interactions, and how FAANGs deploy eBPF safely in production. You should be able to design eBPF-based observability, networking, and security solutions.

eBPF Architecture:

  • BPF program types: XDP (driver-level networking), TC (traffic control), KPROBE (dynamic kernel function tracing), TRACEPOINT (static kernel tracepoints), PERF_EVENT (hardware PMU events), SK_SKB (socket operations), CGROUP_SOCK (cgroup socket ops), LSM (security hooks — BPF LSM since 5.7).
  • BPF verifier: The verifier performs a static analysis of the program before loading. It checks: (1) program terminates (bounded loops only, since 5.3), (2) no out-of-bounds memory access, (3) no null pointer dereference, (4) return value matches context type, (5) helper function argument types match. Complexity limit: Verifier rejects programs with >1M instructions explored (can hit this with complex loops). Control flow: The verifier builds a DAG of all possible paths — max 64 nested calls.
  • JIT compilation: eBPF programs are JIT-compiled to native code (x86-64, ARM64, RISC-V). The JIT compiler translates BPF bytecode to native instructions — typically 1:1 mapping. Performance: JIT-compiled XDP programs run at ~10-25 Mpps per core, ~10-50ns per packet processing overhead.

CO-RE (Compile Once, Run Everywhere): BPF programs must navigate kernel data structures (struct task_struct, struct sk_buff) which change between kernel versions. Traditional approach: Compile BPF on each target machine (requires kernel headers). CO-RE approach: (1) Compile BPF with BPFTypeFormat (BTF) debug info, (2) Record the target kernel's BTF, (3) At runtime, libbpf relocates the BPF program's field accesses to match the running kernel's struct layout. BTF: BPF Type Format encodes type information in a compact binary format. Since Linux 5.2, BTF is shipped with the kernel (CONFIG_DEBUG_INFO_BTF). Staff+ insight: CO-RE was a breakthrough — before it, every kernel update required recompiling all BPF tools. Now, tools like bpftrace, BCC, and Cilium ship once and work across kernels.

FAANG eBPF Deployments:

Katran
Meta's L4 load balancer — XDP + BPF, 20M+ pps/server
Cilium
Kubernetes CNI — eBPF for service mesh, security, networking
BPF for DDoS
Cloudflare — L4 DDoS mitigation via XDP, dropping at line rate
sections.codeBlock:
# eBPF diagnostics and deployment

# Check BPF system limits
bpftool feature | grep -E 'map|prog|limit'

# Increase BPF limits for production
sysctl -w kernel.bpf_max_progs_per_cgroup=200

# Load a BPF program (example: XDP drop)
ip link set dev eth0 xdp obj drop.o sec .text

# List loaded BPF programs
bpftool prog list

# Show BPF map contents
bpftool map dump id <map_id>

# CO-RE: compile with BTF
clang -g -O2 -target bpf -c program.bpf.c -o program.o
# libbpf handles relocation at runtime

sections.title: Virtualization & Containers: KVM, cgroups, Namespaces, gVisor & Firecracker
sections.description:
VIRTUALIZATION · CONTAINERS · HYPERVISORS

Staff+ expectation: FAANG infrastructure runs on virtualization and containers. You must understand the tradeoffs between hardware VMs (KVM), lightweight VMs (Firecracker), containers (cgroup v2 + namespaces), and sandboxed execution (gVisor). Each has different security, performance, and density characteristics.

KVM — Kernel-based Virtual Machine: KVM turns Linux into a type-1 hypervisor by exposing /dev/kvm — userspace (QEMU, cloud-hypervisor) can create VMs with hardware acceleration (Intel VMX / AMD SVM). Key components: (1) KVM.ko — core module, handles VMX transitions, (2) kvm-intel.ko / kvm-amd.ko — architecture-specific, (3) QEMU — userspace device emulation (storage, network, virtio-blk/virtio-net). Performance: KVM adds ~5-10% overhead for compute, ~10-30% for I/O (virtio). Nested virtualization: KVM supports nesting — running KVM inside a KVM VM (used for cloud CI/CD pipelines). FAANG use case: Google Compute Engine, AWS Nitro (KVM-based), Azure. Staff+ insight: The biggest KVM performance issue is exit frequency — every VM-exit costs ~1-5µs. Devices that cause many exits (emulated IOAPIC, PIT) must be optimized. Use paravirtualized devices (virtio) and in-kernel IRQ chip to minimize exits.

cgroups v2 — The Unified Hierarchy: cgroups v2 (unified since 5.0) provides resource isolation for containers. Key controllers: cpu (weight/max), memory (current/low/high/max/oom_group), io (weight/max for read/write), pids (max process count), cpuset (CPU/memory node affinity), rdma (RDMA resources). cgroup v2 vs v1 differences: (1) Unified hierarchy — no more multiple hierarchies, (2) No task migration — all threads in a cgroup move together, (3) PSI (Pressure Stall Information) — per-cgroup CPU/memory/IO pressure metrics, (4) Memory.reclaim — write to trigger reclaim without OOM. FAANG practice: All FAANGs use cgroup v2 in production. Google's Borg uses a predecessor; Kubernetes (since 1.24) defaults to cgroup v2.

Namespaces — Lightweight Isolation: Linux namespaces isolate global resources: PID (process IDs), NET (network devices, IP, ports), MNT (mount points), USER (UID/GID mapping), UTS (hostname), IPC (System V IPC, POSIX message queues), CGROUP (cgroup namespace — hides parent cgroups), TIME (CLOCK_MONOTONIC, CLOCK_BOOTTIME offsets). Key insight: Namespaces alone do not provide security — --privileged containers or those with CAP_SYS_ADMIN can escape. Seccomp-bpf (secure computing mode with BPF) filters syscalls — essential for container security. FAANG practice: All container runtimes at FAANG use seccomp-bpf with a default deny-all syscall filter, whitelisting only required syscalls.

gVisor — Sandboxed Container Runtime: gVisor (Google's OSS sandbox) intercepts application syscalls and handles them in a userspace kernel (Sentry). Architecture: (1) Application syscalls are intercepted by a ptrace or KVM-based platform, (2) Sentry reimplements ~60% of the Linux kernel in Go (scheduler, networking, file systems, mmap), (3) Unsafe or unsupported syscalls return ENOSYS. Performance: ~10-50% overhead vs native containers, but strong isolation — a kernel exploit in the Sentry doesn't affect the host. Tradeoff: Not all applications work (FUSE, ptrace, some io_uring features). FAANG use case: Google Cloud Run and App Engine use gVisor for multi-tenant serverless.

Firecracker — MicroVM for Serverless: Firecracker (AWS Nitro, OSS) is a VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) optimized for serverless workloads. Key features: (1) Minimal device model — only virtio-net, virtio-block, serial console (no VGA, ACPI, BIOS complexity), (2) MicroVM bootstrap in ~125ms, (3) 5MiB memory overhead per microVM, (4) KVM-based hardware isolation. Why Firecracker vs containers? Container breakout via a single syscall can compromise the host. Firecracker's HW-isolated VMs provide stronger security boundaries — a kernel exploit inside the VM only compromises that VM. FAANG use case: AWS Lambda and Fargate run on Firecracker (~10M+ microVMs per region).
sections.codeBlock:
# Container and VM tuning at FAANG scale

# cgroup v2 PSI monitoring — detect resource pressure
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/<group>/cpu.pressure
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/<group>/memory.pressure
cat /sys/fs/cgroup/<group>/io.pressure

# Seccomp-bpf default whitelist for containers
# Docker's default seccomp profile allows ~300 of ~450 syscalls
docker run --security-opt seccomp=default.json

# KVM tuning — reduce VM-exit frequency
# Use in-kernel IRQ chip (kernel_irqchip=on)
# Use paravirtualized KVM clock (kvm-clock)
# qemu-system-x86_64 -machine accel=kvm,kernel_irqchip=on ...

# Firecracker microVM config
# { "vm": { "vcpu_count": 2, "mem_size_mib": 1024 } }

sections.title: Kernel Tracing & Observability: perf, ftrace, BCC & bpftrace
sections.description:
TRACING · OBSERVABILITY · PROFILING

Staff+ expectation: You must be fluent in kernel observability tools. Debugging a production performance issue means choosing the right tool: perf for CPU profiling, ftrace for latency tracing, BCC/bpftrace for ad-hoc eBPF analysis, and flamegraphs for visualization. You should know the cost of each tool and how to minimize overhead in production.

perf — The Swiss Army Knife: perf uses hardware PMU (Performance Monitoring Unit) counters and software tracepoints. Key use modes:

  • CPU profiling: perf record -F 99 -a -g -- sleep 60 — samples all CPUs at 99Hz with call-graphs. Overhead: ~1-3% at 99Hz. Intel PEBS (Precise Event-Based Sampling) gives better accuracy.
  • Hardware counters: perf stat -e cycles,instructions,cache-misses,branch-misses — aggregate counts with minimal overhead.
  • Tracepoint events: perf record -e sched:sched_switch -a — tracks context switches with wakeup latency.
  • Off-CPU analysis: perf record -e sched:sched_stat_sleep,sched:sched_switch -a -g -- sleep 60 — identifies why threads are blocked (I/O, lock contention, voluntary sleep).

ftrace — Function Tracing: ftrace (Function Tracer) records kernel function calls with microsecond timestamps. Key files in /sys/kernel/tracing/: available_tracers (function, function_graph, preemptirqsoff, wakeup, etc.), current_tracer (select tracer), set_ftrace_filter (trace specific functions), trace (read output). Function graph tracer: Shows function entry/exit with duration — echo function_graph > current_tracer. Staff+ insight: ftrace's function tracer adds ~10-50% overhead (depending on function call rate). Use set_ftrace_filter to limit to specific functions in production. The irqsoff tracer is invaluable for finding sources of scheduling latency — traces the maximum time interrupts are disabled.

BCC & bpftrace — eBPF-Based Tracing:

ToolLanguageBest ForOverhead
BCCPython + C (eBPF)Complex tools (opensnoop, execsnoop, tcptrace, biolatency)Low once loaded (eBPF in kernel)
bpftraceawk-like DSLAd-hoc one-liners (single kernel function tracing)Low (~1-5% for targeted probes)

Flame Graphs — The Standard Visualization: Invented at Netflix by Brendan Gregg. Procedure: (1) Sample with perf record -F 99 -a -g -- sleep 60, (2) Fold call stacks: perf script | stackcollapse-perf.pl > out.folded, (3) Generate SVG: flamegraph.pl out.folded > flame.svg. Reading flame graphs: Width = CPU time (proportional sampling), height = stack depth. Wide frames at the top are leaf functions consuming CPU. Color coding: Random colors for threads/processes, or red for the hottest paths. FAANG practice: Flame graphs are generated continuously in production at Netflix, Meta, and Google — with automated regression detection (a new flame graph that adds 2% CPU to a known function triggers an alert).
sections.codeBlock:
# Kernel tracing commands at FAANG scale

# CPU profiling with perf (99Hz sampling)
perf record -F 99 -a -g -- sleep 60
perf report --stdio -g folded  # text-based call tree

# Off-CPU analysis (why is my thread blocked?)
perf record -e sched:sched_stat_sleep,sched:sched_switch -a -g -- sleep 60

# ftrace: trace specific kernel function
echo function > /sys/kernel/tracing/current_tracer
echo do_sys_open > /sys/kernel/tracing/set_ftrace_filter
cat /sys/kernel/tracing/trace

# bpftrace: trace memory allocations
bpftrace -e 'kprobe:__alloc_pages_nodemask { @[comm] = count(); }'

# Generate flame graph
perf script | stackcollapse-perf.pl > out.folded
flamegraph.pl out.folded > flame.svg

sections.title: Performance Tuning & Capacity Planning
sections.description:
PERFORMANCE · CAPACITY · TUNING

Staff+ expectation: Performance tuning is systematic, not guesswork. You must apply the USE method (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) to every resource, build capacity models, and understand the latency distributions that matter (p50/p99/p999 vs mean). You should know the standard sysctl blueprint for a Linux host and when each tuning knob helps or hurts.

The USE Method — Every Resource, Every Time: Developed by Brendan Gregg: For every resource (CPU, memory, disk, network, interconnects), check: Utilization (% busy over time), Saturation (queued work exceeding capacity), Errors (count of error events). Application to CPU: Utilization = /proc/stat (sum of user/nice/system/irq/softirq/steal), Saturation = load average (number of tasks in uninterruptible + run queue), /proc/schedstat, perf sched latency, Errors = process crashes, panic. Application to memory: Utilization = /proc/meminfo (Active + Inactive), Saturation = major page faults, swap usage, PSI memory pressure (avg10 > 10), THP compaction stalls, Errors = OOM kills. Application to disk: Utilization = iostat %util (but misleading for SSDs — use avgqu-sz and await instead), Saturation = IOPS vs max IOPS, PSI io pressure, Errors = I/O errors in /sys/block/*/device/eh_cnt.

Sysctl Blueprint — Production Linux Host:

Staff+ insight: There is no universal tuning — every workload is different. But the following baseline is a starting point for a latency-sensitive serving host. The key is understanding which knobs matter and which are cargo-culted. For example, tcp_tw_recycle is widely cargo-culted but removed in 4.12. swappiness=1 is cargo-culted from Redis docs but doesn't apply to all workloads. Below is a reasoned baseline:

  • VM settings: vm.swappiness=10 (avoid swap, but don't set to 0 — that disables anon page reclaim), vm.dirty_ratio=5 (low for latency-sensitive; 20% default causes writeback bursts), vm.dirty_background_ratio=2, vm.vfs_cache_pressure=200 (aggressively reclaim dentry/inode cache — freed memory > cost of cache miss), vm.min_free_kbytes=67584 (for 256GB host — reserve 64MB for atomic allocations).
  • THP: madvise mode — explicit advice via MADV_HUGEPAGE / MADV_NOHUGEPAGE per application.
  • Network: net.core.rmem_max=134217728 (128MB), net.core.wmem_max=134217728, net.core.netdev_budget=600 (more packets per softirq), net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control=bbr, net.ipv4.tcp_fastopen=3.
  • Kernel: kernel.numa_balancing=0 (applications manage NUMA), kernel.sched_min_granularity_ns=3000000 (3ms min preemption — reduce context switch overhead for batch), kernel.sched_wakeup_granularity_ns=4000000.

Latency Distributions — Mean Lies: Average latency hides tail latency. At FAANG scale, the p99.9 latency determines user experience (1 in 1000 requests is slow). Latency decomposition: Network round-trip, queueing delay, kernel time (syscalls, context switches, interrupts), application processing, garbage collection. Tooling: perf stat for syscall counts, strace -T -c for syscall duration distribution, bpftrace for application-level latency histograms. FAANG practice: Every microservice exposes latency histograms (p50, p90, p99, p99.9, p99.99) in 100ms buckets. SLOs are based on p99 latency over 1-minute windows. If p99 exceeds SLO for 5 consecutive minutes, on-call is paged.
sections.codeBlock:
# Production sysctl baseline for latency-sensitive host

# Memory
vm.swappiness = 10
vm.dirty_ratio = 5
vm.dirty_background_ratio = 2
vm.vfs_cache_pressure = 200
vm.min_free_kbytes = 67584
vm.page-cluster = 0

# THP (Transparent Huge Pages)
echo madvise > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
echo madvise > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/defrag

# Network
net.core.rmem_max = 134217728
net.core.wmem_max = 134217728
net.core.netdev_budget = 600
net.ipv4.tcp_congestion_control = bbr
net.ipv4.tcp_fastopen = 3
net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle = 0

# Scheduler
kernel.sched_min_granularity_ns = 3000000
kernel.sched_wakeup_granularity_ns = 4000000

# Disable NUMA balancing
kernel.numa_balancing = 0

sections.title: OS Security Hardening
sections.description:
SECURITY · HARDENING · LSM · TPM

Staff+ expectation: Secure the OS against container escapes, kernel exploits, supply chain attacks, and physical attacks. At FAANG scale, the threat model includes: a compromised container trying to escape, a malicious insider with physical access, a compromised build pipeline injecting backdoors, and zero-day kernel exploits.

LSM — Linux Security Modules: LSM provides a framework for security policy enforcement. SELinux (NSA, 2000): Label-based mandatory access control (MAC). Each process (subject) and object (file, socket, IPC) has a security context. Rules define allowed operations (e.g., httpd_t can write to httpd_log_t). AppArmor (Canonical, 1998): Path-based MAC. Profiles specify file access, network access, and capabilities per executable. Staff+ insight: SELinux is more expressive (type enforcement, multi-category security), but AppArmor is simpler to configure. FAANGs use SELinux for host-level isolation and AppArmor for container profiles. BPF LSM (Linux 5.7+): eBPF programs can implement security policies attached to LSM hooks — allows dynamic, programmable security without kernel module compilation.

IMA & dm-verity — Integrity Measurement: IMA (Integrity Measurement Architecture): Measures file content hashes and stores them in a TPM-protected PCR (Platform Configuration Register). At boot, IMA measures every executable before it runs. At attestation time, a remote verifier checks IMA log against expected hashes. dm-verity: Device-mapper target that provides transparent integrity checking of block devices (used by Android's verified boot, ChromeOS, and container images). The device is divided into blocks; each block's hash is stored in a Merkle tree. Reads are verified against the tree — tampered blocks return I/O error. FAANG use case: Both are used in Amazon's Bottlerocket OS and Google's Container-Optimized OS — the root filesystem is dm-verity protected, and IMA measures all container images at launch.

Kernel Lockdown — Restricting Kernel Access: Lockdown mode (Linux 5.4+) restricts access to kernel features that could be used to subvert the kernel. Modes: integrity (prevent modifying the running kernel — no kexec, no hibernation, no BPF modification of kernel code, /dev/mem/mkm restricted), confidentiality (integrity + prevents reading kernel memory — no /dev/kmem, no kprobes, no BPF reading kernel data via bpf_probe_read). How to enable: lockdown=integrity or lockdown=confidentiality on kernel command line. FAANG practice: Production hosts run with integrity lockdown. confidentiality is used for PCI-DSS environments.

Live Patching — Kpatch, KGraft, Livepatch: Kernel live patching allows applying security fixes without rebooting. How it works: (1) A stop_machine operation pauses all CPUs, (2) The function's first instruction is replaced with a jump to a new function (using ftrace or a custom trampoline), (3) CPUs resume — all new calls go to the patched function. Limitations: Only functions can be patched (not data structures). Cannot patch functions that are on the stack of a running thread (these will still execute the old version). FAANG practice: Meta uses Kpatch for critical CVEs — they ship live patches within hours of a CVE disclosure and apply to their fleet without reboots. Google uses KGraft (similar). Kernel live patching saves millions in lost compute (a fleet reboot costs ~$100K per 10K servers for lost uptime).
sections.codeBlock:
# OS security hardening at FAANG scale

# Enable SELinux enforcing mode
setenforce 1  # runtime
# /etc/selinux/config: SELINUX=enforcing

# Kernel lockdown (kernel cmdline)
# lockdown=integrity

# IMA measurement policy
# ima_policy=appraise_tcb ima_appraise=enforce

# dm-verity for root filesystem
veritysetup open /dev/sda1 verity_root /dev/sda2

# Kernel live patch (kpatch)
kpatch load kpatch-cve-2024-*.ko

# Disable unprivileged BPF
kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled = 1

# Restrict kernel pointer exposure
kernel.kptr_restrict = 2
kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1

sections.title: OS for AI/ML Workloads: GPU Scheduling, RDMA & CPU Partitioning
sections.description:
AI/ML · GPU · RDMA · HPC

Staff+ expectation: AI training and inference workloads place unique demands on the OS. You must understand GPU scheduling (NVIDIA vs AMD, MIG partitioning, GPU time-slicing), RDMA networking for distributed training, CPU partitioning for inference, and the memory hierarchy challenges of large model serving.

GPU Scheduling & Isolation: Three levels of GPU sharing: (1) Time-slicing — GPU context switches between processes (NVIDIA MPS / CUDA streams). Overhead: ~10-20µs context switch. Limited isolation — a single process can saturate GPU memory. (2) MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) — NVIDIA A100/H100 hardware partitioning. Each GPU can be split into up to 7 instances with dedicated memory, cache, and compute units. Staff+ insight: MIG gives hardware-level isolation — one MIG instance cannot affect another's performance. But MIG is rigid (fixed partitions: 1/7, 2/7, 3/7, 4/7, 7/7 of A100). (3) vGPU (SR-IOV) — GPU virtualization with dedicated vGPUs per VM. Each vGPU has guaranteed slices of compute and memory. Used in cloud GPU instances (AWS g5, Azure NVv4).

400Gbps
NVLink 4.0 bandwidth (H100, ~600GB/s effective)
~2µs
RDMA (InfiniBand) one-way latency
7
Max MIG partitions per A100

RDMA — Remote Direct Memory Access: Distributed training (FSDP, DeepSpeed) uses all-reduce collectives for gradient synchronization. RDMA enables GPU-to-GPU direct data transfer without CPU involvement. Implementation options: (1) InfiniBand — native RDMA, 400Gbps HDR, ~1-2µs latency. (2) RoCE v2 (RDMA over Converged Ethernet) — RDMA over Ethernet (requires PFC — Priority Flow Control — to prevent packet loss). GPUDirect RDMA: GPU memory can be directly registered for RDMA — no staging through CPU memory (saves one copy per transfer, ~5-10µs per message). Staff+ insight: At 10,000 GPU training scale, all-reduce synchronization becomes the bottleneck. Meta and Google use ring all-reduce with optimized NCCL (NVIDIA Collective Communications Library) that uses NVLink for intra-node and InfiniBand RoCE for inter-node. The OS must pin NIC interrupts to NUMA-local CPUs and ensure GPU memory is allocated on the same NUMA node as the NIC.

CPU Partitioning for Inference: ML inference (especially LLM serving) has asymmetric workload: GPU does matrix multiplications, CPU handles tokenization, scheduling, and KV cache management. OS considerations: (1) NUMA-aware CPU pinning — pin model worker threads to CPUs on the same socket as the GPU, (2) Core isolation — reserve cores for GPU management (NVIDIA's nv-hostengine, CUDA driver threads) and isolate them from application threads via isolcpus, (3) Memory bandwidth — LLM inference is memory-bandwidth bound — ensure CPU-to-GPU copy path doesn't share memory controller with other tenants. FAANG practice: Google's TPU v4 pods use a custom OS configuration with dedicated CPU partitions for data loading (which overlaps with computation). Meta's inference stack uses cpuset cgroups to reserve cores for GPU management and isolate serving workers.
sections.codeBlock:
# GPU and RDMA tuning for AI/ML workloads

# NUMA-aware GPU pinning
# Ensure GPU and NIC are on same NUMA node
nvidia-smi topo -m

# Pin process to GPU's NUMA node
numactl --cpunodebind=0 --membind=0 ./training_script.py

# RDMA tuning — increase max RDMA memory registration
sysctl -w vm.nr_hugepages=32768  # 64GB of 2MB pages

# Set RDMA request queue sizes
# /sys/class/infiniband/mlx5_0/resource/rc_qp_max=4096

# CPU isolation for inference
echo 0-3 > /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset/gpu_management/cpuset.cpus
echo 0 > /sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset/gpu_management/cpuset.mems

sections.title: CXL & Memory Disaggregation
sections.description:
CXL · MEMORY DISAGGREGATION · FABRIC

Staff+ expectation: CXL (Compute Express Link) is the most important hardware evolution for data center memory architecture. You must understand CXL's protocol (CXL.io, CXL.cache, CXL.mem), how it enables memory pooling and disaggregation, and the OS changes needed to support tiered memory (NUMA-like management for CXL-attached memory).

CXL Protocol Overview: CXL runs over PCIe 5.0/6.0 physical layer. Three protocols multiplexed over the same link: CXL.io (PCIe-based I/O — discovery, enumeration, DMA), CXL.cache (coherent caching — accelerator can cache host memory), CXL.mem (memory access — host can access accelerator-attached memory with cache coherency). Key capability: CXL.mem enables memory pooling — a CXL memory controller can expose a pool of memory shared across multiple hosts. Latency: CXL-attached memory adds ~50-100ns compared to local DDR5 (~100ns local vs ~150-200ns CXL). This is faster than NVMe (10µs+) but slower than local NUMA (100-200ns).

OS Support for Tiered Memory: CXL-attached memory appears as a new NUMA node. Linux kernel support: (1) NUMA emulation — CXL memory nodes are reported as NUMA nodes with higher latency than local DRAM, (2) kmem driver — manages CXL-attached memory as regular memory, (3) DAX (Direct Access) — byte-addressable persistent memory support applies to CXL memory too. Staff+ insight: The OS must decide where to allocate memory — local DRAM for hot data (active working set), CXL for warm/cold data. Auto-tiering: Linux can use numa_maps with migration thresholds (similar to NUMA balancing) to promote hot pages from CXL to local DRAM. Meta has demonstrated demotion of cold pages to CXL-attached memory, saving 30%+ on DRAM costs for caching workloads with only ~5% p99 latency increase.

Memory Disaggregation — The Data Center Memory Bus: Instead of provisioning memory per-server, memory disaggregation pools all memory in the rack (or cluster) and allocates it dynamically. Benefits: (1) Utilization improvement: Average server memory utilization is ~60% — pooling reduces stranded memory. Google estimates 25% fleet-wide memory savings with disaggregation. (2) Independent scaling: CPU and memory scale independently — compute-heavy ML training doesn't need to pay for unused DRAM slots. (3) Faster failure recovery: A failed server's memory is still accessible from the pool. Challenges: (1) CXL latency penalty (50-200ns extra), (2) CXL switch bandwidth (need 2-4TB/s per rack), (3) OS page migration overhead. FAANG interest: Google, Meta, and Microsoft are all deeply invested in CXL. Meta deployed CXL memory pooling in production for cache workloads in 2024. Google's TPU v4 pods use a form of disaggregated memory. AWS has CXL-based instances in preview.
sections.codeBlock:
# CXL memory configuration

# CXL-attached memory appears as NUMA node
numactl --hardware  # shows CXL NUMA nodes

# Check CXL device
ls /dev/cxl/*

# DAX device for CXL memory
ndctl create-namespace -m devdax -e namespace0.0

# Tiered memory policy: prefer local for hot data
numactl --preferred=0 ./workload  # prefer NUMA node 0 (local DRAM)

# Demotion to CXL memory (warm pages)
# /sys/kernel/mm/numa/demotion_enabled = 1

sections.title: Cloud OS: Nitro, Bottlerocket, Container-Optimized OS & Unikernels
sections.description:
CLOUD OS · HYPERVISOR · MINIMAL OS

Staff+ expectation: Cloud providers have built specialized operating systems that strip away everything not needed for containerized workloads. You should understand the architecture of AWS Nitro, Bottlerocket, Google's Container-Optimized OS, and the emerging unikernel trend — and be able to argue when a custom OS is worth the operational complexity.

AWS Nitro — The Hypervisor-as-a-SoC: Nitro is not just a hypervisor — it's a hardware-software system that offloads virtualization overhead to dedicated hardware. Architecture: (1) Nitro cards — dedicated hardware for VPC networking (ENI), EBS storage, and NVMe controller. These are PCIe-attached SoCs that handle I/O without consuming host CPU cycles. (2) Nitro hypervisor — lightweight KVM-based hypervisor that runs on the host CPU. Key advantage: No device emulation (no QEMU). The Nitro cards present native NVMe and ENA (Elastic Network Adapter) devices to the guest — the guest OS sees real hardware, not emulated devices. Staff+ insight: This eliminates a major KVM overhead — device emulation in QEMU causes ~10-30% I/O overhead. Nitro's HW offload brings virtualized I/O to near-bare-metal performance. The Nitro security model: The Nitro card firmware is validated at boot; the control plane (API calls from AWS) is cryptographically authenticated. Even Amazon engineers cannot SSH into a Nitro host without breaking attestation.

Bottlerocket — Minimal OS for Containers: Amazon's Bottlerocket is a Linux distribution purpose-built for running containers. Key design decisions: (1) Immutable root filesystem — the rootfs is dm-verity protected and mounted read-only. No package manager, no SSH by default (sheltie admin container provides access). (2) Two-partition update model — active and inactive root partitions. An OS update downloads to the inactive partition, then a reboot flips the boot target. No init system complexity — uses runc directly (not Docker daemon), managed by thar-be-settings and host-containers. (3) Tiny footprint — ~200MB root filesystem (vs ~1GB for Ubuntu Server). Security: No compilers, no interpreters (except admin container), no sudo, no setuid binaries except apiserver. FAANG use case: Bottlerocket runs on Amazon EKS and ECS nodes (millions of containers).

Google's Container-Optimized OS (COS): COS is Google's minimal OS for GKE nodes. Similarities to Bottlerocket: Immutable root, no package manager, atomic updates. Differences: (1) Uses systemd for service management, (2) Default SSH via OS Login (IAM-based), (3) Includes Google's guest environment for GCE metadata and networking. Tooling: COS provides cos-tool for debugging — runs in a privileged container with host namespaces.

Unikernels — The Ultimate Specialization: Unikernels compile the application with only the OS primitives it needs into a single bootable image (e.g., MirageOS, OSv, Nanos). Benefits: (1) Boot time in milliseconds, (2) Image size in megabytes, (3) No context switching (single process), (4) No privilege separation (entire system runs in ring 0 — no syscall cost). Challenges: (1) Limited to simple applications (no fork, minimal threading), (2) Device drivers must be ported, (3) Debugging is harder (no standard tools). FAANG use case: Google's gVisor (described above) is not a unikernel but a userspace kernel — another approach to minimizing OS overhead. AWS's Firecracker microVMs bridge the gap between containers and unikernels. True unikernels are not yet deployed at FAANG scale, but elements appear in NFV (Network Function Virtualization) appliances at carriers.
sections.codeBlock:
# Bottlerocket OS management

# Check active partition
cat /etc/os-release

# Update to new OS version
# Bottlerocket updates via TUF (The Update Framework)
apiclient update check
apiclient update apply

# Admin container access
echo '{"username": "admin", "password": "<password>"}' | \
  apiclient -m post /api/admin/access

# COS (Container-Optimized OS) debugging
# Run debug container with host access
gcloud compute instances add-metadata <instance> \
  --metadata=cos-debug-container=true

sections.title: Staff+ Interview Strategy & System Design with OS Depth
sections.description:
INTERVIEW PATTERNS · STRATEGY · DEPTH

Staff+ expectation: OS questions in Staff+ interviews never ask you to define a page table. Instead, they embed OS depth into system design questions. The interviewer wants to see you reason about the OS implications of architectural decisions. This section maps common Staff+ system design questions to their OS underpinnings.

Common Staff+ System Design Questions with OS Depth:

  • "Design a high-throughput key-value store (like Redis/Memcached)" — OS angles: (1) I/O model — epoll vs io_uring for networking, (2) Memory management — THP on/off (Redis recommends disabling THP due to jemalloc + COW fork issues), huge pages for TLB efficiency, (3) NUMA pinning — pin Memcached to NUMA-local memory to avoid remote access penalty, (4) OOM protection — set oom_score_adj = -1000, use memory.high for proactive reclaim, (5) Kernel by-pass — would DPDK or XDP help? (Probably not for in-memory stores where bottleneck is memory bandwidth, not packet rate.)
  • "Design a container runtime" — OS angles: (1) Syscall filtering — seccomp-bpf default deny, (2) Namespace isolation — PID, NET, MNT, USER, (3) Resource limits — cgroup v2 cpu.weight/memory.high/io.weight, (4) Security — gVisor vs Firecracker vs plain runc, (5) Image layering — overlayfs vs btrfs vs devicemapper, (6) Init process — tini or dumb-init for proper signal handling.
  • "Design a global file storage system" — OS angles: (1) Local filesystem choice — XFS for large-scale (Netflix), ext4 for general use, Btrfs for snapshots, (2) Direct I/O vs buffered — databases use O_DIRECT to manage their own cache, (3) Writeback tuning — dirty_ratio/dirty_expire for latency-sensitive workloads, (4) io_uring — for high-IOPS data plane, (5) NUMA-aware page allocation — ensure data buffers are local to accessing CPU.
  • "Design a fault-tolerant distributed lock service (like ZooKeeper/etcd)" — OS angles: (1) WAL durability — fsync frequency and tradeoffs (every write vs batch), (2) Page cache impact — heavily written WAL can evict hot cache, (3) Disk scheduler — none for NVMe, deadline for SATA SSD, (4) I/O priority — use ionice to prioritize WAL writes over background compaction, (5) Memory overhead — each Raft log entry consumes kernel slab memory.
  • "Design a serverless platform (like AWS Lambda)" — OS angles: (1) MicroVM design — Firecracker's minimal device model, 125ms boot time, (2) Overcommit — memory overcommit with cgroup -EAGAIN on exceed, (3) Network isolation — VPC with ENI trunking, (4) Cold start optimization — pre-warmed sandboxes, fork-based snapshots (Meta's SEV-SNP approach), (5) Seccomp profile management — shared seccomp filter cache.

Build vs Buy Decision Framework: (1) When to build a custom kernel? Google builds google-gentoo, Meta customizes their kernel — this is justified at 100K+ server scale where a 1% efficiency gain saves millions. (2) When to use a minimal OS? Bottlerocket or COS for container-native workloads; standard Ubuntu/Debian for general-purpose. (3) When to use kernel bypass? DPDK/XDP for packet processing at >10M pps; io_uring for storage at >100K IOPS; epoll is sufficient for most microservices (<10K connections).

Operational Excellence Signal: The strongest Staff+ candidates reveal that they've been woken up at 3 AM for OS-related incidents. Stories about debugging OOM kills, THP compaction latency, NUMA imbalance, or kernel panics with kdump show real depth. Always tie OS knowledge to operational impact: "We reduced p99 latency by 40% by moving from epoll to io_uring for our key-value proxy — but we had to upgrade the kernel across 20K hosts, which required a 3-month rollout."

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
Staff+ OS engineering requires reading deeply from both kernel source and production engineering experience. Below are the canonical references organized by topic.

TopicResourceWhy for Staff+
FoundationalOperating Systems: Three Easy Pieces (Remzi & Andrea Arpaci-Dusseau, free online at ostep.org)The modern OS textbook. Chapters on processes, scheduling, memory virtualization, concurrency, and persistence are essential. Staff+ engineers should know Chapters 4-35 cold.
Linux InternalsLinux Kernel Development (Robert Love, 3rd ed) & Understanding the Linux Kernel (Bovet & Cesati, 3rd ed)The Linux kernel books. Process scheduler, memory manager, VFS, I/O subsystem — every Staff+ infrastructure engineer should have read both cover-to-cover.
PerformanceSystems Performance (Brendan Gregg, 2nd ed) & BPF Performance Tools (Brendan Gregg)The definitive guides to Linux performance analysis. The USE method, flame graphs, profiling methodology — every Staff+ engineer's operating manual.
VirtualizationKVM Forum talks (annual, YouTube) & Firecracker internals (AWS OSS blog)KVM internals from the maintainers. Firecracker's minimal device model is a masterclass in OS specialization.
eBPFBPF and XDP Reference Guide (cilium.readthedocs.io) & Linux BPF man pagesThe most comprehensive online resource for BPF. Covers BPF architecture, program types, maps, CO-RE, and the verifier.
SchedulingEEVDF Scheduler (LWN.net, 2023) & Linux kernel sched/ sourceUnderstanding the CFS→EEVDF transition is essential for Staff+ OS conversations. Read the LWN article series and the kernel commit messages.
MemoryTHP and Production (Meta OSS blog) & CXL 2.0 Specification (computeexpresslink.org)Meta's learnings on THP tuning is the best operational reference for memory management. CXL spec for memory disaggregation understanding.
IOio_uring and Linux 5.x (LWN.net, 2019-2023) & DPDK Programmer's Guideio_uring is replacing epoll and AIO. The Lord of the io_uring series by Jens Axboe is essential. DPDK for kernel bypass understanding.
Cloud OSNitro: Hypervisor (AWS re:Invent) & Bottlerocket OS design (AWS OSS blog)AWS Nitro is the industry's most advanced hypervisor. Bottlerocket is the model for container-optimized OS design.
